coredata.dc:title: Coupled nonlinear stress and electric field numerical simulation for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kodama, M.
Horikawa, N.
Ohashi, A.
Hirai, S.
coredata.dc:description: An accurate analysis of ion transportation in an all-solid-state battery is crucial to improve and estimate performance. For an all-solid-state battery using sulfide solid electrolytes, coupling the stress and electric field simulations is required because the battery is pressurized, this affects the void space, which then affects ion transportation. In this paper, we propose a new method to analyze the ionic conductivity in a pressurized all-solid-state battery by coupling nonlinear stress analysis and electric field analysis. The nonlinear stress analysis can estimate the stress distribution of solid electrolyte and it was found that nonlinear stress-strain characteristics that originate from the voids diminished with increased pressure. The numerical results based on a model-electrode were nearly identical to the experimental results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-solid-state battery
Nonlinear stress analysis
Numerical simulation
X-ray computed tomography
Young's modulus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the coupled nonlinear FEM and FVM simulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the X-ray CT cell: 1. Upper stainless-steel rod, 2. Sample, 3. PEEK cylinder, 4. Lower stainless-steel rod, 5. Spring, 6. Duralumin body, 7. Screw. (b) Numerical model measured using X-ray CT in the experiment (zirconia volume ratio of 60%).
(a) Strain as a function of stress. (b) Young's modulus as a function of stress. (c) Ionic conductivity as a function of stress measured in the experiment.

Experimental and numerical results at an applied pressure of 200 MPa. (a) Strain of dummy electrode as a function of zirconia ratio. (b) Relative ionic conductivity as a function of zirconia ratio normalized by ionic conductivity with zirconia ratio of 0%.
Numerical results of (a) material distribution, (b) stress, and (c) ionic conductivity distributions of composites with zirconia ratio of 30% and 60%.

head.ce:title: Coupled nonlinear stress and electric field numerical simulation for all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tokyo Institute of Technology, NE-8 2-12-1 Meguro, Tokyo, 152-8550, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tokyo Institute of Technology, NE-8 2-12-1 Meguro, Tokyo, 152-8550, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An accurate analysis of ion transportation in an all-solid-state battery is crucial to improve and estimate performance. For an all-solid-state battery using sulfide solid electrolytes, coupling the stress and electric field simulations is required because the battery is pressurized, this affects the void space, which then affects ion transportation. In this paper, we propose a new method to analyze the ionic conductivity in a pressurized all-solid-state battery by coupling nonlinear stress analysis and electric field analysis. The nonlinear stress analysis can estimate the stress distribution of solid electrolyte and it was found that nonlinear stress-strain characteristics that originate from the voids diminished with increased pressure. The numerical results based on a model-electrode were nearly identical to the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:para: All-solid-state lithium-ion battery technology is attracting attention because of its high safety and energy density resulting from the inorganic solid electrolyte (SE) used for ion transportation [1,2]. In the case of a bulk type all-solid-state battery with sulfide SE, electrodes are made by mixing the active material with other assisting materials. High pressure is applied to the mixed powder during the manufacturing process because ionic conductivity of the sulfide SE increases with applied pressure [3–5]. For a high-performance battery (high charging speed and high battery capacity), the lithium ions in the electrode must be smoothly transported to achieve low overpotential. This is achieved through optimization of the electrode structural design, which is conducted both experimentally and numerically.

For numerical design optimization, a high accuracy numerical method is required, and the numerical method should be able to simulate both the powder-electrode compression and ion transportation in the electrode. There are typically two methods of numerical simulation for powder compression, Discrete Element Method (DEM) [7] and Finite Element Method (FEM) [8]. DEM treats the powder as an aggregate of spheres and directly solves the particle-particle interactions [6]. Giménez et al. simulated the calendaring process of a lithium-ion battery cathode using DEM and discussed the influence of calendaring on the electrode structure [7]. This method can simulate the interaction between particles and voids directly by calculating the movement, contact, and slip of the particles. However, DEM cannot solve the stress distribution inside the particles. Moreover, for three-dimensional numerical simulations with high accuracy, a large number of powder particles must be simulated, which requires high computational costs and estimating many parameters (e.g. rebound coefficient, static friction coefficient, rolling friction coefficient). FEM treats particles of powder and voids as a continuous body, which is then divided into smaller elements. Each element is described by a series of mechanical equations that are then solved [8]. FEM cannot solve the interaction between particles directly, but does have a smaller calculation cost than DEM because the simulation resolution (i.e. element size) can be much larger than the particle size of the powder making up the SE. FEM also requires fewer parameters than DEM for simulation.

In a previous study, stress and ionic conduction in a dummy electrode (zirconia ​+ SE) were simulated using linear FEM stress analysis coupled with electric field simulation using a finite volume method (FVM) [5]. The zirconia in the dummy electrode has a comparable Young's modulus to LiCoO2 but is electrochemically stable. Moreover, the lithium ionic conductivities of LiCoO2 (∼0.01 mS/cm) and zirconia (smaller than LiCoO2) are much smaller than that of sulfide SE (∼1 mS/cm) [9], so the ion transport in an electrode is mainly conducted by the SE in both electrodes of the dummy electrode with SE and zirconia and an actual electrode with SE and LiCoO2. Therefore, the dummy electrode with SE and zirconia can simulate only the mechanical characteristics of a cathode with LiCoO2 and the ion transportation characteristics of the SE network. In that study, the ionic conductivity of the dummy electrode was successfully simulated using the coupled simulation. However, the numerical results have a large degree of error. This is because the stress simulation is conducted using a linear FEM calculation, in which the Young's modulus of combined material of SE and void must be constant. In reality, the Young's modulus of combined material increases with pressure because the void decreases with increasing stress meaning that the Young's modulus should not be constant.

To conduct an accurate numerical simulation of compression and ionic conduction using FEM, the change in the Young's modulus must be taken into consideration. Nonlinear FEM is one method for increasing the accuracy of the numerical simulation of power compression [10]. In nonlinear FEM, a linear stress analysis is performed iteratively and the Young's modulus corresponding to the strain is applied to each of the elements, the stress distribution is then calculated implicitly. This enables the numerical simulation of a combined material of SE and void with a non-constant Young's modulus. Therefore, coupling a nonlinear stress analysis and electric field analysis provides a highly accurate numerical simulation of ion conduction with reduced error.

In this study, we conduct experiments and numerical simulations for a dummy electrode of an all-solid-state lithium-ion battery. In the experiments, ionic conductivity is measured using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and the electrode structure is measured using X-ray computational tomography (CT). The parameters for the numerical simulation can be determined from the experiments and the data can be used to validate the simulations. The numerical simulation couples nonlinear FEM stress analysis and FVM electric field analysis and the numerical results are compared with the experimental results to evaluate the accuracy of the numerical method. Moreover, we discuss the effect of the stress field on ion transport in the SE (while taking into consideration the nonlinear characteristics of SE as a powder).

Electrode structure is measured using X-ray CT and ionic conductivity is measured using EIS to obtain parameters for numerical simulation and to validate the simulation results. The measurements are taken from a dummy electrode composed of SE and zirconia [5]. Zirconia is used in place of LiCoO2 because of its comparable Young's modulus and electrochemical stability, meaning charging and discharging can be neglected [11].

Li5.5PS4.5Br1.5 developed by Wang et al. [12] was used as the SE. 3 g of the Li5.5PS4.5Br1.5 were prepared using the following method. First, Li2S, P2S5 and LiBr were mixed in a specific ratio in an agate mortar. The mixture was placed in a 50 mL zirconia pot, with16 pieces of zirconia having a particle size of 10 mm, and a grinding aid (0.2 mL diethyl ether). The mixture was mechanically milled with a high energy vibration mill (Emax, Retsch) at 1200 rpm for 10 h. Then, the mixture was heated at 400 °C for 10 h in an electric furnace. All the operations were performed under a low dew point (less than −70 °C) and in a nitrogen atmosphere.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the overview of the cell used in this study. The cell consists of stainless-steel rod, sample, Poly Ether Ether Ketone (PEEK) cylinder, spring, duralumin body, and screw. The diameter of the powder sample is 1.0 mm with a thickness of approximately 1.0 mm. By tightening the screw, the spring inside is compressed upwards and the sample can be pressurized up to 400 MPa. The cell is airtight, ensuring the sample is not exposed to oxygen. The PEEK cylinder and the duralumin body have low X-ray absorptivity, allowing the state of the spring and sample inside the cell to be observed using X-ray CT [13]. X-ray CT measurements are conducted using a laboratory-scale cone-beam X-ray optical system. The X-ray source of the system is 160 kV and 70 μA tungsten target. The voxel size is 5 μm × 5 μm × 4 μm.

To obtain the stress-strain relationship and the stress-ionic conductivity relationship for numerical simulation, an experiment with pure SE (without zirconia) was conducted. The 3D structure and ionic conductivity of pure SE were measured with the X-ray CT and the EIS, respectively, while changing the applied pressure from 0 ​MPa to 400 ​MPa. Experiments with the dummy electrode (SE ​+ ​zirconia) were also conducted with the same method.

The ionic conductivity of the dummy electrode is calculated by coupling stress and electric field numerical simulations. The purpose of this study is to elucidate the effectiveness of nonlinear FEM stress analysis for electrode simulation. Therefore, the electrode structure in the simulations should be the same as that used in the experiments to allow validation. This is achieved by basing the numerical simulation on the X-ray CT-measured structure. Fig. 1 (b) illustrates the numerical model for a zirconia volume ratio of 60%, measured using X-ray CT at an applied pressure of 0 MPa. The gray spheres are the zirconia particles and the yellow region is the SE and void. In the nonlinear FEM stress analysis, the SE and voids are treated as one continuous body. Therefore, in the numerical model (Fig. 1 (b)), the electrode is divided into those two regions.

The numerical model is a cylindrical shape (with a 1.0 mm diameter). The boundary condition for the side of the cylindrical electrode is a sliding wall, and a pressure is applied vertically to the top of the cylinder. The bottom of the cylinder is fixed to the coordinate system. Young's modulus of the zirconia region is set as 200 GPa and is constant. This is because, the yield stresses of LiCoO2 and ZrO2 are about 400 MPa and are higher than the applied pressure of 200 MPa; therefore, plastic deformation was assumed to be negligibly small [14,15]. The Young's modulus of the SE and void region is set using the experimentally measured value and is not constant because of the impact of pressure. The nonlinear FEM simulation is conducted with COMSOL Multiphysics; the MUMPS method is used for the linear system of equations; and Newton's method is used for the nonlinear system of equations. The numerical grid for FEM is tetrahedron shaped with an approximate grid size of 15 μm.

The results of the nonlinear stress analysis (material distribution and stress distribution under pressure) are imported to the in-house FVM electric field analysis code, and the ionic conductivity is subsequently calculated [4,5]. The following equations (steady state Maxwell equations and Ohm's law) are the governing equations for the FVM simulation.
(1–1)
∇ ×
E →
= 0
(1–2)
E →
= − grad
ϕ
(2)
∇ ⋅
j →
= 0
(3)
E →
= σ ⋅
j →
(4)
σ =
{
f
( p )
( f o r
S E
a n d
V o i d
r e g i o n )
0
( Zirconia ​region )

E →
: electrical field vector,
ϕ
: electrical potential,
j →
: ion flux density vector,
σ
: ionic conductivity,
p
: stress,
f
: ionic conductivity as a function of stress measured in the experiment for pure SE.

These equations are discretized by the 2nd order FVM. Poisson's equations for electrical potential are derived and solved using the SOR (Successive Over-Relaxation) method. In eq. (4), the stress distribution is converted to the ionic conductivity distribution. The function of ionic conductivity to pressure is an experimentally measured relationship between stress and ionic conductivity for pure SE. The boundary condition for the side of the cylinder is that it is electrically insulated, and a unit potential difference is applied to the top and bottom. The ionic conductivity can be calculated with the total ion flux from top to bottom. The numerical grid for FVM is hexahedron shaped and the grid size is 5 μm.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the strain as a function of stress measured in the SE powder. These data are obtained from experiments without zirconia (only SE is installed into the X-ray CT cell). The strain dramatically increases in the low stress region (0–50 MPa), and the slope is relatively small in the high stress region (>50 MPa). This stress-strain relationship is comparable to previously reported results in which the void reduction and plastic deformation of SE are occurred in the compression process [3,4,16]. Fig. 2 (b) shows Young's modulus as a function of stress calculated from the stress-strain relationship. As shown in this figure, the Young's modulus is not constant due to the void reduction and the plastic deformation. The Young's modulus of sulfide SE calculated from first principles calculations is approximately 20 GPa at the true density state [17]. However, the measured Young's modulus is not constant, and it increases steadily from 0 to approximately 700 MPa and is much smaller than the calculated value from the first principles calculations. This is because, in this study, SE and voids are treated as one body. In reality, applying pressure decreases voids between SE particles and causes the mixture to deform easily. Therefore, the Young's modulus is small.

The presence of voids causes strain to increase; considering SE and voids as one continuous body increases the strain and decreases Young's modulus. Especially in the low stress region, the volume ratio of void is high; the strain dramatically increases with stress as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and Young's modulus is lower than 100 MPa. Fig. 2 (c) shows the experimentally measured ionic conductivity of SE powder as a function of stress. The ionic conductivity increases with stress because of the decrease of voids between SE particles [4]. An ionic conductivity of 4.0 mS/cm in Li5.5PS4.5Br1.5 at a high stress condition (400 MPa) is comparable to the value measured by Wang et al. [12]. In the nonlinear FEM stress and electric field FVM coupled simulation, the experimentally measured Young's modulus versus stress curve (Fig. 2(b)) and ionic conductivity versus stress curve (Fig. 2 (c)) are used for the calculation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the coupled nonlinear FEM and FVM simulation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the strain in the dummy electrode (zirconia composite) with an applied pressure of 200 MPa as a function of dummy active material (zirconia) volume ratio. The black points are the experimentally measured results and red points are the numerical results. In both experimental measurements and numerical simulation, the strain ranges from 0.3 to 0.5 and decreases with an increase in the volume ratio of the dummy active material. This is because the increase of the volume ratio of the high Young's modulus material (zirconia) constructs mechanical pillars that support the composite, decreasing strain. In Fig. 3 (a) there is a small discrepancy between the experimental and numerical strain results, with the strain calculated numerically being smaller than that obtained experimentally. In the FEM simulation, the contact between the SE and zirconia is rigid and the SE cannot detach from the zirconia. This non-detachment enhances the mechanical strength of the electrode, explaining the discrepancy between the experimental and numerical results. To improve the numerical accuracy with non-linear FEM stress analysis, friction coefficient and bond strength between the SE and zirconia (active material) should be experimentally measured and are inputted as a boundary force between the SE and zirconia (active material).

Fig. 3 (b) shows the relative ionic conductivity of the dummy electrode with an applied pressure of 200 MPa as a function of dummy active material volume ratio. The data of dummy active material volume ratio of 0% are obtained from experiment without zirconia, and data of dummy active material volume ratio from 30% to 60% are obtained from experiment with zirconia. Applied pressure of 200 MPa was selected because it is the typically applied pressure on the cathode of all solid-state lithium-ion battery [18]. The ionic conductivity is normalized by the ionic conductivity of pure SE. The ionic conductivity determined by experiment and numerical simulation are in close agreement. It is important to note the numerical results do not have error bars. In previous studies using linear FEM stress analysis, the reported error bars accounted for the inability to consider nonlinear mechanical characteristics of the SE powder [5]. Here, that error is removed with the inclusion of nonlinear stress analysis. The ionic conductivity decreases with increasing volume ratio because of (1) the increase in the tortuosity of the SE network with the increase of non-ion-conductive material (zirconia), and (2) the decrease in the stress on the SE and void, causing the ionic conductivity of SE and void region to decrease. The slight discrepancy between the experimental and numerical results, seen in Fig. 3 (b), stems from the difference in the strains shown in Fig. 3(a). Therefore, implementing a model that accounts for the detachment of active material and SE would improve accuracy.

Fig. 4 illustrates the numerical results for the distribution of (a) material, (b) stress, and (c) ionic conductivity with an applied pressure of 200 MPa and with zirconia volume ratios of 30% and 60%. The thickness of the electrode is different between the 30% and 60% conditions because the initial thickness when the volume ratio is 30% is smaller in the experiment. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), zirconia pillars are not constructed in the 30% case but are constructed in the 60% case, and the formed pillar decreases the stress in the SE and void region. As shown in Fig. 2 (c), the difference in the stress has a direct influence on the ionic conductivity, accordingly, the numerical simulation found that the average ionic conductivity of the SE and void region is 0.6 mS/cm for the 30% case and 0.4 mS/cm for the 60% case. As discussed above, the decrease in the ionic conductivity of the SE and void regions is one of the factors that causes the ionic conductivity of the composite to decrease when the volume ratio of dummy active material is increased.

In this study, we proposed a new simulation method to estimate the ionic conductivity of an all-solid-state battery electrode by coupling nonlinear FEM stress analysis and FVM electric field analysis. The numerical method is validated using experimental results from X-ray CT and EIS measurements. The nonlinear FEM stress analysis successfully simulated the powder's compression characteristics by simulating the nonlinear stress-strain characteristics of the SE and void region of the electrode, induced by the reduced void volume. The simulated strain is in good agreement with the experimental results and the ionic conductivity calculated using the coupled numerical simulation is in excellent agreement with the experimental results. The numerical simulations show that the ionic conductivity of a composite decreases because of the increase in tortuosity and the decrease of stress in the SE as the volume ratio of active materials with a high Young's modulus is increased.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Japan Keirin Autorace Foundation.
